steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Boundaries question. The sentence reads: "The use of OHD requires careful calibration based on a sample's climatic context and [blank] temperature fluctuations, variations in water vapor pressure, and differences in glass chemistry are known to affect the rate of rind growth." The word "though" must appear after "composition" to introduce a concessive contrast. The correct punctuation must join "composition" to "though" and then correctly introduce the contrasting clause.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — composition, though:: A comma after "composition" correctly sets off "though" as it begins the contrasting idea, and the colon after "though" formally introduces the clause listing the factors that also affect rind growth. This structure is grammatically sound and clearly signals contrast. ✓
  • Choice B — composition though: No punctuation between "composition" and "though" fuses the two clauses without any boundary, creating an unclear run-on. ✗
  • Choice C — composition; though: A semicolon links two independent clauses of equal weight. Here the second clause is a concession introduced by "though," not a coordinate equal statement, so a semicolon is too heavy and misrepresents the grammatical relationship. ✗
  • Choice D — composition, though,: Placing commas on both sides of "though" treats it as a parenthetical aside, which would require that the sentence still read correctly without it. Without "though," the sentence loses its contrasting signal and becomes a run-on. ✗
A is correct.
